candidate.title: Machine-readable conformance test vectors (JSON Schema + vectors)
candidate.description: Create a `spec/conformance/` directory with a JSON Schema defining the test vector format and 30+ vectors covering: valid handshake round-trip, expired agent credential, revoked human identity, stale root (both trees), scope subset violation, cumulative-bit encoding violation, delegation chain at max hops, nonce replay, and the phantom-delegatee attack. Each vector includes inputs, expected circuit output (accept/reject), and the specific error code. This is the minimum viable interop artifact for any alternative SDK implementation to prove compatibility.

candidate.title: Human tree root history buffer parity with agent tree
candidate.description: The IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but has no equivalent for humanTree — human proofs go stale the instant a new enrollment lands. This is a spec-level gap: the IETF draft's Security Considerations section claims 'proofs remain valid across concurrent enrollments' but the contract doesn't enforce it for humans. Add a matching `humanRootHistory[30]` ring buffer, update `verifyHandshake` to accept any root in the buffer, and add a conformance test vector for 'human proof against root N-15'.

candidate.title: Public signal field overflow allows Solidity/circuit semantic mismatch
candidate.description: The IdentityRegistry contract accepts public signals as uint256 and passes them directly to verifier contracts. Circom operates over BN254's scalar field (r ≈ 2^254), but Solidity uint256 goes up to 2^256-1. Values in range [r, 2^256-1] are valid in Solidity but would wrap modulo r in the circuit, creating a semantic mismatch. Specifically, sessionNonce and currentTimestamp are user-supplied public inputs — an attacker could submit sessionNonce = n + r which the contract treats as a different nonce (bypassing usedNonces) but the circuit reduces to n. Add require(pubSignal < r) checks in IdentityRegistry for every public input before calling the verifier, matching the pattern already used inside snarkjs-generated verifiers for proof elements. Write a Certora rule proving no public signal can exceed the scalar field bound.

candidate.title: JWT-encoded off-chain session token for verified handshakes
candidate.description: Define a compact JWT profile (draft-bolyra-session-token) that encodes the verified handshake result — humanNullifier, agentNullifier, scopeCommitment, sessionNonce, and verification timestamp — as a signed JWT. This lets relying parties consume Bolyra handshake results without on-chain queries, using standard JWT libraries. The spec should define registered claims mapping, a `bolyra+jwt` media type, and an `x5c`-style proof-of-verification header pointing to the on-chain tx hash. Aligns with OAuth 2.0 DPoP (RFC 9449) binding semantics for the nonce field.

candidate.title: SD-JWT session tokens for off-chain proof reuse
candidate.description: Every API call currently requires a fresh ZK proof (1-5s). After verifyHandshake() succeeds, the verifier should mint a compact SD-JWT token binding nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, and expiry with selective disclosure and a configurable TTL (60-3600s). Ship `SessionTokenIssuer.mint(handshakeResult)` and `SessionTokenVerifier.verify(token, { requiredClaims })` in the SDK. This eliminates the single biggest adoption blocker for real-time applications.

candidate.title: Self-describing proof envelope with named public signals
candidate.description: Proofs are currently bare `{ proof, publicSignals: bigint[] }` with positional semantics — `publicSignals[0]` vs `publicSignals[4]` is a constant source of integration bugs. Ship a `BolyraEnvelope` type with `encode()`/`decode()` that wraps proofs in `{ version, circuit, provingSystem, signals: { humanMerkleRoot, nullifierHash, ... }, proof }`. Include a `fromRaw(circuit, rawSignals)` migration helper. Publish in both TS and Python SDKs so integrators never index by position again.

candidate.title: Standardized proof envelope with IANA content type registration
candidate.description: Define an `application/bolyra-proof+cbor` content type with a CBOR-encoded envelope wrapping the Groth16/PLONK proof, public signals array, circuit identifier, and proving-system tag. Currently the SDK serializes proofs as ad-hoc JSON with no version field or circuit discriminator — consumers must guess the layout. The envelope adds a 2-byte version prefix, a circuit enum (human/agent/delegation/model-instance), and a proving-system enum (groth16/plonk). Register with IANA per RFC 6838. Include ABNF for the text fallback (`application/bolyra-proof+json`).

candidate.title: Automatic circuit artifact fetching via CDN with integrity checks
candidate.description: The quickstart currently requires manually downloading or building circuit artifacts (r1cs, zkey, wasm) before any proof can be generated — a multi-step process involving Circom toolchain setup. Ship a `@bolyra/artifacts` package that lazily fetches versioned circuit artifacts from a CDN (GitHub Releases or S3) with SHA-256 integrity verification and local caching in `~/.bolyra/artifacts/`. The SDK's `proveHandshake()` should auto-resolve artifacts without user configuration. This removes the biggest onboarding friction: 'I installed the SDK but I can't generate a proof.'
